experiments.title: How adding value prop to the donate button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop to the donate button on Care Net’s main donation page will achieve more donations because it will encourage users to click the donate button. It will remind them why they are giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2025 - 12/08/2025
Care Net’s donation pages typically have a donate button that says “Give Now” under that it says “Amount: $XX.” We were interested in seeing what would happen if we replaced the “Amount: $XX” with additional value prop. We replaced it with “I want to protect life.” We ran this experiment on their main donation page because that’s where they see the majority of their traffic and we knew this test would have a better chance of validating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated the powerful impact of mission-driven language on donor behavior. By replacing the default “Amount: $XX” label on Care Net’s highest-traffic donation page with a value-based statement — “I want to protect life” — the test reframed the giving decision from a financial transaction to a personal expression of identity and belief. The treatment produced a 44% lift in conversion rate, increasing from 18.7% to 27.0%, with 99.3% statistical confidence, confirming that the effect was both meaningful and reliable.
This result suggests that donors are more likely to complete a gift when the call to action connects directly to their values and sense of purpose, rather than focusing on dollar amounts or mechanics. In essence, emphasizing the “why” of giving at the point of decision appears to activate emotional motivation and a sense of alignment with the organization’s mission. For Care Net, this insight reinforces the importance of using identity-based and mission-oriented framing not just in broader messaging but in microcopy across key conversion points. Future tests could explore how different variations of purpose-driven language—tailored to distinct donor motivations—further influence giving behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a separate “Give Monthly” button on the website menu impacts donation conversions and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Give Monthly” button for the main website will achieve an increase in donations and overall revenue because it increases clarity for people wanting to make a gift to Care Net.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2023 - 12/20/2023
Care Net has a robust recurring donor program that has grown significantly over the years. Recently, due to a variety of factors, they have seen a plateau in the conversion of recurring donors.
On their main website, they have a “Donate” button in the top right-hand corner. For this test, we wanted to see how adding a “Give Monthly” button next to the “Donate” button impacted three factors: one-time donations, recurring donations, and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Give Monthly" button alongside the existing "Donate" button on the main website resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 74.8% increase in revenue compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.4%.

This suggests that the presence of a "Give Monthly" button provided clarity for people wanting to make a recurring donation, leading to a higher conversion rate and ultimately more revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of different call-to-action buttons and messaging on donation conversion. This could involve testing variations of the button design, placement, and wording to determine the most effective combination. Additionally, analyzing the data from this experiment can provide insights into the specific donor segments that responded most positively to the "Give Monthly" button, allowing for targeted marketing efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How a landing page redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a landing page redesign for content offer viewers from acquisition will achieve the same email conversion rate because the landing page messaging and experience will mostly stay the same.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 08/05/2024
With the launch of a new website, we considered moving landing page platforms but wanted to get the data before deciding to transition in case it impacted results. We built a treatment in the new platform and compared how visitors responded in comparison to the old platform. The treatment kept all of the same copy from the control, but had some differences in formatting which may have impacted results. We ran the two versions side by side and split traffic for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesign of the landing page for content offer viewers from acquisition led to a significant decrease in email acquisition. The treatment performed 32.3% worse than the control, with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the original landing page outperformed the redesigned version. We believe this is due to a few distinct differences in the user experience on the page, including checkboxes, width of form fields, presentation of headline, formatting of page copy, and color and copy on the CTA button. While on the surface the pages were essentially the same, these slight changes in design made a significant impact on results and reduced email conversion.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to landing pages, even if the messaging and content remain the same. Small differences in formatting and design can have significant effects on user behavior and conversion rates. It is also essential to thoroughly test any new designs before fully implementing them, to avoid negative impacts on conversion rates. Additionally, considering running qualitative research such as user testing and surveys in addition to A/B testing to gather more insights into how users are interacting with the landing page and to understand their preferences and behavior better.

experiments.title: How an animated icon on the monthly tab of a donation page impacts monthly donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form, that defaults to monthly, and has an animated heart icon for donation page vistors will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the animated heart makes it more appealing to look at.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2024 - 04/23/2024
Care Net has “Donate” and “Give Monthly” buttons in the header of their website. They both lead to different donation pages. The “Give Monthly” button leads to a page that defaults to a monthly gift with a checkmark. We wanted to test that using a tabbed donation form, that defaults to monthly, and has an animated heart icon. We think it could increase monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the tabbed donation form with an animated heart icon, which defaulted to monthly giving, did not lead to the expected increase in monthly gifts. The treatment group experienced a 100.0% decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 96.0% confidence level. Even though the experiment is considered valid, we should retest this because there were only four conversions total.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis that the animated heart icon would make the donation form more appealing and lead to an increase in monthly gifts. Future experimentation should focus on testing different elements and factors that could genuinely appeal to donors and encourage recurring donations. It is crucial to continuously test and iterate on donation forms to find the most effective design and messaging for encouraging donors to give monthly.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the length of a survey impacted clicks on a donor email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about the length of a survey for donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated. because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2023 - 09/08/2023
When sending out a survey to donors, we wondered if including the number of questions in a survey within an email would enhance its appeal and clarity for recipients. We believe that by stating the number of questions upfront, recipients can gauge the time commitment required, making the survey seem more manageable and less daunting. This transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling. It assures recipients that their time is valued and that the survey won’t be overly lengthy or burdensome.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about the length of a survey can significantly increase survey participation. By clearly communicating the number of questions upfront, recipients are able to gauge the time commitment required and make an informed decision about participating in the survey.

The treatment group in this experiment experienced a 47.6% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the transparency about the survey length was effective in capturing recipients' attention and motivating them to click. Additionally, the Clicks to Opens metric showed a substantial 42% increase, further suggesting that recipients found the survey more appealing and were more likely to engage with it.

To utilize this learning in future experimentation, it is important to continue incorporating transparency and clear communication in the design of survey invitations. Including information about the number of questions or the expected time commitment can help set realistic expectations and encourage more recipients to participate. It is also worth exploring other ways to enhance the appeal and clarity of survey invitations, such as emphasizing the value and impact of the survey or providing a brief overview of the topics covered.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of transparency and clear communication in engaging donors and increasing survey participation. By incorporating these elements in future experimentation, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our survey invitations and ultimately gather more valuable insights from our donors.

experiments.title: How the placement of a content offer on blog article pages impacts email sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-out style interrupter to promote an eBook offer on blog article pages will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it will capture the reader’s attention mid-article instead of having to scroll to the bottom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2023 - 10/30/2023
Care Net has typically run what we call a “dear reader” style ad at the end of their articles. For example, the reader would be on a blog article page, read the entire article and then after, would be presented with what looks like a continuation of the article but starts with, “Dear reader,” and then presents the content offer to the reader.
We were curious to see how many people weren’t actually scrolling to the bottom of the article and therefore, weren’t seeing the content offer. So we decided to develop a slide out that used the same copy and also included the same image of the eBook, but would be presented to the reader once they scrolled through 50% of the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a slide-out interrupter significantly increased the number of emails acquired for all traffic by 638.1% compared to the traditional "dear reader" style ad at the end of the article.
2. The slide-out interrupter was successful in capturing the reader's attention mid-article, eliminating the need for them to scroll to the bottom to see the content offer.
3. The slide-out interrupter was able to effectively promote the eBook offer, leading to a higher conversion rate.
4. The results of the experiment were statistically significant at a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a strong likelihood that the treatment was the cause of the increase in email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using the slide-out interrupter on blog article pages. This approach has proven to be more effective in capturing the reader's attention and increasing email acquisition. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the timing or design of the interrupter to optimize its effectiveness further. Furthermore, considering the success of the interrupter in promoting the eBook offer, it could be applied to other content offers or calls to action on the website to improve conversion rates.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clearly communicating the length of a survey for non-donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because of the enhanced appeal and clarity of the time and effort involved..
experiments.summary:

2. Providing the number of questions in the survey helps recipients gauge the time commitment required, making it seem more manageable and less daunting.

3. Transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling.

Based on these learnings, future experimentation should continue to focus on clear communication and setting expectations with recipients. Exploring other elements of the email or survey content that could enhance click-through rates can be valuable for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA impacts donor conversion during a Fiscal Year End Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar notifying visitors of the threat of the federal abortion hotline for Care Net’s Pregnancy Decision Line will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and fear of loss.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/30/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
Historically, they’ve used a dear reader at the bottom of article pages with messaging around the urgent need in the pro-life space and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their fiscal year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. The control and the treatment both had the same copy, but the placements were different: the control being at the end of the article and the treatment being at the beginning. On the treatment, once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, the dear reader would then turn into a sticky bar with the same copy.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not have a federal, taxpayer-funded hotline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which consisted of an in-line appeal that became a sticky bar with the same copy when scrolling, performed significantly worse than the control. It resulted in a 70.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this design approach did not effectively convert visitors into donors.
2. Different visitor segments showed even larger decreases in donations. Returning visitors had an 828% decrease. These significant drops in donation rates suggest that the in-line appeal with the fear of loss did not resonate well with these specific segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the Wikipedia "before article appeal" approach used in the treatment did not effectively appeal to visitors to become donors. The results suggest that a different messaging strategy should be tested in future experimentation.
Instead of relying solely on the placement, other emotional appeals or value-driven messaging could be considered. For example, highlighting how many more people can be reached through the Care Net Pregnancy Decision Line might be a more compelling appeal for potential donors, or showcasing how this new federal hotline could impact people being able to find life-affirming care.
Additionally, it may be worth exploring different placements for the appeal. While the treatment placed the messaging at the beginning of the article, future experiments could test different placements such as the end of the article, in a sidebar, or in a pop-up overlay.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a Facebook ad impacts conversion and cost.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2018 - 02/02/2018
Care Net offers a curriculum for churches to start a pro-life ministry called Making Life Disciples. To get more people to sign up for the curriculum, they pushed the offer through paid Facebook ads. They developed a control that had a quote on it about God being able to make abortion unthinkable through the church. They hypothesized that they might be able to motivate more people to convert if they gave more context in the ad through more value proposition copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment significantly increased conversion by 859%. This tells us that when we provide more context, communicate the value of the offer, and have a call-to-action in the text copy of our ads, we can increase conversion. The expanded value proposition not only got more people to click through to the landing page, but these people were much more highly motivated to convert. This treatment increased conversion, but even more, it decreased cost per conversion substantially from $32/conversion to $5.30/conversion.

Our hypothosis was that the high text-to-image ratio of our Weekend Update email, which had two images in the body and a large footer image, caused Gmail to treat the email as a marketing message rather than a “personal” one.
experiments.keyLearnings: Increasing the text-to-image ratio of an email significantly increase the number of people who saw the message, increasing the email's open rate by 36%. While images in emails may be more common, they are rarely found in "personal" messages and thus detract from engagement when attempting to send a personalized marketing message.

We wanted to determine what type of subject line would best lead subscribers to open the email. A subject line that was more conversational or one that simply stated that the message was the latest “Care Net Connect” message.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more conversational subject line significantly increased the open rate compared to a more informational one. experiments.title: How making an end-user click “See More” in a Facebook ad impacts click-through to the acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would increase click-through and ultimately name acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 09/07/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. They use paid Facebook ads to push the offer. Through previous testing, we had learned that longer Facebook ad copy increases name acquisition. To build upon those learnings, we tested ad copy that was even longer resulting in the end-user having to click “See More” to read the entirety of it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version with the extended copy increased click-through by 82%! What this tell us is that people are not only more highly engaged with long ad copy, but even longer ad copy requiring people to click “See More” increases engagement and results in more people clicking through to the landing page and converting. The “See More” version provided additional information that the end-user needed to make the decision to click-through to the landing page and ultimately sign-up for the course.

experiments.title: The impact of a standard Facebook event vs a custom event on volunteer signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using Facebook’s registration conversion event for our volunteer campaign will achieve greater conversion rates and lower cost-per-signup.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/13/2021
For more than a year, we’ve been running a campaign on Facebook to acquire volunteers for our Making Life Disciples initiative. We had an ad that pointed to a pledge signup page. Those who signed the pledge would then land on a volunteer signup page. We had been using a custom conversion that targeted the volunteer confirmation page. We knew from our donation experiments that using the standard “purchase” event in Facebook increased conversions when tested against a custom conversion targeting our donation pages. We wondered if a similar principle would work with testing Facebook’s standard “registration” conversion event against our custom conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at email acquisition, we increased signatures on the pledge (step 1) by 155% by using the standard event. Now, signatures that came through the custom event converted to signups at about a 30% increased rate but we only had 60% validity on that metric. Since we were getting so many more users through step 1 using the standard conversion, even at a lower conversion rate on step 2, we still converted more pledge signers into volunteers. We also reduced our cost per signup from $37 to $15 by using this standard event.
It is possible that Facebook's algorithm was unable to collect enough conversions on the custom event to properly optimize for it. But either way, the standard event was clearly better and led to more emails acquired and a lower volunteer signup conversion cost.

experiments.title: How the order placement of the credit card fields impacts conversion on a primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the credit card form fields later in the donation pathway for donors and prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 12/03/2020
On Care Net’s primary donation page, we have been focused on optimizing the donation form in an effort to increase donor conversion. Through past research studies, and in working with many different non-profit organizations, we have found that the order in which information is acquired on the donation form varies greatly. To better understand the optimal order placement of the form fields on the page – specifically the credit card section – we decided to test it with Care Net. Care Net’s primary donation page asks for the credit card information immediately after they ask for the gift amount. This piece of information would be the second step in the donation pathway. We hypothesized that we may be able to increase donor conversion if we asked for that piece of information at the very end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By moving the credit card section to the very end of the giving form, we increased donor conversion by 36% on desktop devices. There was little to no impact on mobile devices.

Revenue experienced a 112.2% increase with a 95% level of confidence.
25-34 visitors had a 700.0% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How optimizing for Subscriptions on Facebook impacts recurring donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will optimizing Facebook ads for “Subscriptions” increase recurring donors when compared to “Purchases?”
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2020 - 06/20/2020
Roughly a year ago, Care Net ran an experiment to determine the impact of using different optimization methodologies within Facebook. In that experiment, we learned that optimizing our Facebook campaigns to acquire “Purchasers” yielded more donors than optimizing for ”Custom Conversions” (aka emails) or even for optimizing for “Donors” as Facebook defines them. Our hypothesis for this result is that Facebook’s advertising algorithm incorporated online buying behavior with ”Purchase” optimization that the other metrics did not. Ultimately, this experiment drastically improved acquisition efforts and significantly lowered costs.
Recently, Facebook rolled out additional conversion options that are available for optimization. One of those options is a ”Subscription” which is intended to represent an ongoing monthly payment. Given Care Net’s emphasis on acquiring recurring donors, we decided to run an experiment to see if this metric could acquire more recurring donors than “Purchase” optimization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full month, we found that by optimizing for Subscriptions (AKA, recurring gifts), we actually decreased the number of recurring gifts that we received by 43%. Our hypothesis is that when Facebook optimizes for Purchases, it incorporates online spending into its algorithm which increases the likelihood that someone the ad targets would make a financial transaction. With Subscriptions, it would appear that this same financial information in not incorporated into the results.

Additionally, this experiment had a negative impact on several other conversion factors
Decreased total donors by 50.9% with a 99.8% LoC
 	Decreased the annual the number of gifts (assuming 12 gifts per recurring gift) by 42% with a 99.9% LoC

experiments.title: How visually presenting the donate button differently impacts traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase traffic to the donation page by visually changing the look of the donate link in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2020 - 06/12/2020
On Care Net’s primary navigation, they provide a link that says “Donate”. This link takes people to their donation page where people can go and make a gift to support the organization. We hypothesized that presenting the link as a button and as an animated button might increase visual presence and ultimately increase traffic to the donation page resulting in more gifts and revenue. To test this, we developed two variations. The first variation was simply making the donate link, look like a button. For the second variation, we added subtle animation to the button that had the heart of the Care Net logo pulse once and the effect ripple across the rest of the button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two weeks, we found that by changing the donate link to a button negatively impacted clicks to the donation page. Additionally, the pulsing donate button had neither a positive or negative impact on clicks. We weren't able to validate the experiment off of donor conversion on the donation page, but we did we a directional 17% decrease in conversion from variation one - which is ultimately due to the significant decrease in traffic going to the page.
We hypothesize that the donate button saw such a negative impact in clicks because the use of buttons on their website, and across the majority of websites, is overused. People may actually be turned off by this. We believe this hypothesis to be true because of other testing we have done with the Care Net audience where we would test out of button and instead use hyperlink text. This would reinforce why the control with the underlined word "Donate" performed best in this experiement.

experiments.title: The impact of Aligning Call to Action Text with the Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad video’s CTA perform better if aligned with the final donation goal of the ad, or with the ad’s headline?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2020 - 02/26/2020
We launched a new pledge to acquire monthly donors on Facebook. For this pledge, we decided to test the text in the ad video. Prior experiments showed us that videos performed better than images in our Facebook campaigns. So we wanted to find out what type of CTA in the video would work best. One that was the same as the headline of the ad, or one that was more aligned with the donation ask that would follow the pledge the ad invited people to sign.
We decided to do a 3-way test between “Sign the Pledge” “You can help save a baby…” and “Do you oppose abortion?”
experiments.keyLearnings: The "You can help save a baby from abortion" version received the most ad clicks. It also converted the most clicks to pledge signers and acquired the most new monthly donors (11 vs 8 on the control and 9 on the other variation). While the ad's headline invited viewers to sign the pledge, the "you can help save a baby" aligned viewers with the ultimate CTA of the donation page as well as appealed to their pro-life sentiments. They wanted to sign the pledge because they opposed the radical new pro-choice laws being passed and wanted to save babies from abortion. We are going to take this learning and see how we can incorporate even greater alignment in the ad copy with the donation page text.

experiments.title: How increasing the personal relevancy of the offer to the reader affected online course registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Can course registrations be increased by causing the reader to imagine their personal emotions and providing a clear solution in response to these feelings?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2019 - 09/24/2019
Care Net was offering a free online course. They crafted a landing page that was very informative, provided specific details about the length and content of the course, and used social proof to show both the number of course takers and their high ratings of the course. However, they wondered if the rest of the language was creating distance between the reader and the intended action of registering for the course. We crafted another version of the landing page that sought to strategically close the distance between the reader and the intended course registration in two ways. First, this new landing page was more conversational in nature, which allowed the reader to imagine themselves experiencing the emotions that were described (“Do you find yourself getting nervous or fearful…”, “Do you feel unsure…”). Second, the treatment presented a very direct solution to combat these emotions  (“You don’t have to feel this way…we want to equip you”) and related it personally to the reader (“This course will give you confidence”). Finally, the the more personal approach in tone and copy in the treatment allowed the reader to connect more intimately with the organization’s cause.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy produced a 14.6% lift in conversion rate with a 99.7% level of confidence. This shows how important it is to connect the reader to the offer by being more personal in tone and increase their perception that the offer can help address their need by clearly showing them how it will impact them.
This approach makes the offer more clearly desirable by ensuring that the reader will see a noticeable impact to a specific emotion or experience in their own life by following the call-to-action.

While the results had not yet reached 95% certainty, the consistent improvement of the new language coupled with the prior testing of similar language on the Defending your pro-life beliefs course which also performed better, caused us to choose a winner.

Previous testing on Facebook had shown that having “Learn more” as the headline worked best for name conversion. But Care Net hypothesized using a more direct headline that gave more clarity to the end-user might work better. They tested the two against each other.

When the headline/call-to-action is too vague, like “learn more”, we create more uncertainty for people and decrease the likelihood of them converting once they get to the landing page.

experiments.title: How the kind and tone of cultivation emails sent can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal tone improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/02/2019
Care Net sends a weekly cultivation email to donors and nondonors on their file. They call this email their “weekly update” and it usually includes a couple of interesting articles or podcast episodes they encourage people to read or listen to. It’s sent every Saturday morning from Ardee Coolidge – a familiar sender to their file as he is also normally the sender of appeal emails. We know that cultivation is critical to the success of an online fundraising program, but we questioned if we made it more personal in nature and changed elements of what was being sent, if we could increase engagement and ultimately donor conversion of their email file.
To test this, we split their file in half – having an equal number of donors and nondonors in each segment, and ran a longitudinal test over a three month period that ended during their fiscal year-end. The control segment continued to receive the standard cultivation/communication and the treatment segment received similar content, but the approach was slightly different. We created a more intentional, high-touch email that used personal tone copy and presented people with one piece of content to read or listen to. We also changed the sender of the treatment emails over the three month duration of the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new approach to the cultivation emails resulted in a 27% increase in donor conversion from nondonors over the three month longitudinal test. This increase in donor conversion can be attributed to a couple of factors: the more personal approach in tone and copy, the change in sender, and presenting people with one call-to-action.
These three factors helped to strengthen our messaging, created a higher touch feel to the communications they were receiving, and connected people to the organization more intimately. This increased people's motivation to give when provided the opportunity.
Simple language, conversational-style copy, and one-to-one communication can have a significant impact on an email file and reinforces the fact that people give to people.

experiments.title: How a Clear Call to Action Impacted Monthly Donor Conversion on a Donation Page

rows.name: Clear 365 Days a Year Value Proposition

experiments.researchQuestion: Will stripped down value proposition with a clear impact CTA increase the number of monthly donors acquired on our donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 08/13/2019
For several years, Care Net has used the same value propositional language for our monthly donor acquisition page.  Every January, we would send out a series of appeals seeking to raise new monthly donors, donors we called “Caring Partners.” As we prepared for the January 2019 campaign, we revisited the donation page and realized that the copy was full of organizational language that may add friction. We wondered if anyone really wanted to become a “caring partner” or even knew what that phrase meant. We also saw that we had copy dedicated to selling a list of benefits to becoming a monthly donor and wondered if those benefits really mattered. Did people become monthly donors because they wanted a “dedicated Care Net team member to serve them?” We stripped out these sections of the page and radically redesigned the copy. We boiled down the value proposition into one key statement: Save babies 365 days a year by becoming a monthly donor. We kept the copy focused on the donor and the impact of their gift of $1/day. We linked it to a concrete number of lives saved by monthly donors since 2008. We broke out the impact of the monthly gift using bullet points. We did not set up a straight AB test, however we had historic data from the page we used the prior campaign that we could use to compare to the new redesigned page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page performed far and above the old one. It was so successful, that we kept using it in a wide variety of campaign offers which is why the traffic was so much higher than the control page. It is important to note that the variation had a conversion rate around 15% during the January campaign, the lower conversion rate reflects the average of all visitors to the page between Jan-August.
Few people are excited about becoming a monthly donor because of "benefits" Care Net provides. They want to save babies 365 days a year. This new value proposition was clearer and more appealing.

experiments.title: How using the Facebook purchase event impacts instant donor conversion

rows.name: Choosing Life Course Launch - new approach

experiments.researchQuestion: Would the purchase event in Facebook help increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 02/25/2019
Care Net offers people the opportunity to take a free online course called, Choosing Life. This online course gets promoted through paid Facebook ads. We have continually been optimizing the acquisition funnel in an effort to decrease name and donor acquisition cost and increase conversion. While we have been able to significantly impact name conversion, instant donor conversion hasn’t been as strong. All of the Facebook campaigns were set up using Facebook’s custom conversion event. This event was based on the email form submission. We hypothesized that testing this conversion event with Facebook’s purchase conversion might help improve instant donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using Facebook's purchase conversion, we were able to significantly impact instant donor conversion. In fact, this conversion event increased instant donor conversion by 661%! The results of this experiment dramatically changed the method in which we view and execute paid acquisition campaigns moving forward. The purchase conversion allows Facebook to pull in additional purchase data to optimize the campaign instead of relying on a singular conversion metric that we tell it to do.

This time, the test would be run during one of our CYE appeals when we find donor response to be relatively high.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Once again, the CTR on the cut the cord email was higher than that of the control email (this time by 38%). Yet, even with shorter copy on the donation page, there were no gifts on the variation. Our control meanwhile brought in six donations. 

The fact that cut the cord was unable to get a single donation during our CYE campaign, while getting more clicks, indicates that the introduction of a second CTA (cutting spending on entertainment) introduced too much friction for the donor. We hypothesize that the increased CTR stems from the uniqueness causing people to click through to learn more about it.

experiments.researchQuestion: How would the placement of the offer in the article impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2018 - 05/25/2018
Care Net learned through previous testing the kind of content that works best for an acquisition offer in the article pages of their blog. They learned that they can turn their blog traffic into names and potentially donors for their organization by offering people the opportunity to sign the Pro-Life 101 Pledge and start their online course. After learning this, they hypothesized that the placement of the offer in the article might impact conversion. To test this, they developed a treatment that placed the acquisition offer in the middle of the article instead of at the end of it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By placing the offer in the middle of the articles instead of at the end of them, they increased name conversion by 39%! This was a bit surprising as we had seen with tests similar to this the opposite result. What we can take away from this experiment are a couple of things:
1. The placement of the offer mid-article was disruptive enough to the reader that it caught their attention and appealed to their beliefs. 
2. The pledge offer has a low cost to the reader and doesn't require a big commitment from them. 
3. The pledge at the end of the article on the control may have gotten too "lost" in the busyness of the other items in the footer of the articles.
4. By offering it higher on the page we were possibly able to catch a higher volume of people who were reading the article at that point than the number of people who actually finished the article. 
5. This leads us to examine how much of the articles people are reading and propose a new round of experiments that will help keep people engaged and on the page for a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How focusing the value proposition on one primary message impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2018 - 05/08/2018
Care Net offers a free online course called, Pro-life 101. After a person signs up for the online course, they are presented with an instant donation ask. In reading the copy on the instant donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion if we focused the value proposition solely on the donation instead of both the details of the course people just signed up for AND the donation. To do this, we developed a treatment that put the details of the course in the subhead of the page and focused the body of the copy on the donation ask. We also reformatted the page to make it easier to read by bolding specific phrases of copy and shortened the body copy from large paragraphs into shorter paragraphs that were bulleted. Finally, to reinforce the impact of a person’s gift, we also told people the number of people they could reach with a specific dollar amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment donation page increased donor conversion by 64%! It mentally walked a person through the decision to make a donation and focused the value proposition on the donation ask. This strengthened the message and more people were motivated to make a gift.

experiments.title: How using an inline form for a particular kind of offer on the homepage impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an inline form on the homepage increase conversion for the pro-life pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2018 - 05/12/2018
On the Care Net homepage, they offer people the opportunity to sign a pro-life pledge. In a previous experiment where they offered people a free online course, we found that a button increases conversion over an inline form. As we thought through the kind of offer they were now giving people – a pledge to sign instead of an online course, we hypothesized that the button approach might not be the best method. To test this, we developed a treatment that added an inline form to the offer on the page. No other content or copy changed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased name acquisition by 32%. The results from this experiment are opposite of the results from a similar experiment for the pro-life online course. Why is that? Because the pro-life course had a much higher mental cost and commitment. People needed more information about the online course before they were ready to sign up for it. That is why a button instead of an inline offer performed better for the course. The pro-life pledge requires a  much lower commitment. People will either align or not align with the position of a pledge. 

What we can take away from this experiment is that we need to be mindful of the kind of offer we have to determine the acquisition call-to-action method.

experiments.title: How adding a pledge to the front of an online course impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding a pledge to the front of the online course increase conversion of course sign-ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2018 - 03/14/2018
Care Net offers a free online course called, Pro-life 101. They have been testing and optimizing this campaign since it started last summer. Through this testing, they have been able to cut down the cost of acquiring a new course sign-up in half. Since then, they have done additional testing but have not been able to impact acquisition cost any more. We proposed a radical experiment for the course that would add a pro-life pledge to the beginning of the course in an effort to decrease the initial cost to get people to the course sign-up page. Once on the sign-up page, we also removed additional form fields that weren’t needed and passed through a person’s email address from the pledge page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By putting a pledge in front of the online course, not only were we able to acquire names initially for much lower ($0.42 a name) through the pledge, we were able to increase course sign-ups by 172%! In addition, we were also able to decrease acquisition cost for the course significantly by 66%. This significant improvement and lift can be attributed to a few things:

    1.  The lower perceived cost of signing a pledge attracted more people to this offer resulting in more people seeing and reading the value prop for the course. 
    2.  The pledge acted as a micro-yes in the conversion funnel leading them to the online course as the next logical step
    3.  By removing the additional form fields for the online course and pushing a person's email address through from the pledge, we reduced significant friction in 
    the sign-up process. 

Since this experiment, all traffic has been pushed towards this approach to get more people signed up for the online course and we will continue to test and optimize this new course experience.

experiments.title: How the deliver method for a content offer can significantly impact acquisition.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a slide-out feature increase email conversion? How would it impact specific audiences and devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2018 - 02/21/2018
On the Care Net site, they offer a couple of content offers to people coming to their site. This is a great way to acquire emails using their site traffic. The offers are currently being delivered through exit-intent pop-ups. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase email conversion if we used a different delivery method. We proposed testing a slide-out feature on their site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By presenting the content offers with a slide out vs an exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase email acquisition by 296%! We found that the treatment was more impactful on individual audiences. Specifically, on mobile and traffic visitors, we saw the slide out have a statistically significant 870% lift on email acquisition. On the flip side, the desktop version had a 184% lift. We wanted to dig into these individual audiences because the experiences for the Control version varied by device; this is due to an exit intent not working on a mobile phone. In those situations, they would see a banner at the top of the screen as they scrolled down. Given this situation, the significant increase makes more sense due to the disruptive nature of the slide out when compared to a small banner at the bottom.

We will re-evaluate this tactic a few months from now. This type of acquisition can begin to decrease in effectiveness as visitors get used to seeing it. We want to make sure we are maximizing the return from organic website visitors.

experiments.title: How an offer is presented on a homepage impacts email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version would increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2017 - 08/23/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. To take advantage of their site traffic and convert those visitors into names and potentially donors, they put this content offer on the homepage of their website. They tested two different ways to present the offers. One way was telling people about the course and having a button that said, “Get Started Today!”. The other way was telling people about the course and having an inline form for people to sign up and take the course right on the homepage. They did a three way test with the control, course offer with button and a course offer with an inline form to see which version would increase email acquisition from their homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Simply drawing more attention to the course on the homepage was not enough to effect a statistically significant difference in course registrations (conversions). We had to draw attention in a way that did not present the ask to register (form fields) too quickly. Thus, it was the combination of getting someone’s attention and an additional page (with additional value proposition information and form fields) that resulted in the greatest possible result.
This ultimately teaches us that the value proposition for the course is complex and we need to achieve mini-yeses in the acquisition process. In this case, the “Get Started Now” button was a mini-yes in the larger funnel to convert more people with the online course.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition to the homepage donate button actually led to a decrease in donations, contrary to the initial hypothesis. Moving forward, it is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the value proposition language before drawing any conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that future experiments reach a statistically significant level of confidence (at least 95%) in order to make informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Will adding the value proposition to the mobile number field harm conversion?

For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of phone numbers that donors are giving us on the episodic donation page by adding some value proposition copy under it. In doing so, we also wanted to see if by adding the copy, it could end up increasing trust and transparency within the organization and result in more transactions.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition under the mobile number field for the main episodic donation page did not yield the intended results. In fact, there was a significant decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 85.6%. This indicates that the control version without the added value proposition performed better in terms of transactions.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of any changes made to donation pages before implementing them broadly. It is also crucial to conduct thorough testing and analysis to ensure that any modifications do not have a negative impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different value propositions or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with donors.

Further testing is recommended to validate the results of this experiment and to gain a better understanding of the factors that influence donor behavior on the donation page. By conducting additional experiments and gathering more data, Care Net can make informed decisions about how to optimize their donation pages for increased donations and donor trust in the future.

experiments.title: How an autonomy-supportive treatment impacted clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing more autonomy  for the housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there will be an enhanced level of motivation that follows the increase in the copy’s autonomy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2024 - 07/09/2024
When running a fiscal year end campaign, we were curious if giving the email readers more autonomy in the email would lead to higher motivation to give and increase clicks in the email.
First and foremost, autonomy support begins with an orientation. As much as we might wish to reduce autonomy support to an algorithmic set of tactics (e.g., offering choice, avoiding pressure or guilt), true autonomy support starts with an attitude: we are not persuading donors, but rather inviting them to support a mission we believe they would value, and informing them about the ways they can help.
Having said that, when we start with “make this match-back or premium more autonomy supportive,” we are already a few steps behind. Within the “autonomy paradigm,” one would not even offer a premium or match-back to entice or seduce donors.
Nonetheless, we believe our treatment copy has made the appeals less pressuring or controlling and, in some instances, supportive of autonomy. This is demonstrated by the removal of controlling language such as “I still need your help” and “We need you”, simplifying sentences to enhance readability and clarity, using “your support” and “your contribution” to emphasize the reader’s autonomy, maintaining urgency without using pressuring language, reducing redundant phrases to streamline the appeal, preserving the core message and call to action, replacing the sense of urgency with a focus on making the strongest effort as the campaign comes to a close, and emphasizing the reader’s autonomy and the impact of their contribution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the impact of introducing more autonomy in emails for a fiscal year end campaign at Care Net showed that the treatment version resulted in a 57.5% decrease in clicks. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% so the results are not valid.
One key learning from this experiment is that while the hypothesis that increased autonomy in email copy would lead to higher motivation and increased clicks seemed plausible, the actual results did not support this. It is essential to recognize that autonomy support is more than just the language used in the emails; it is about the overall attitude of inviting donors to participate in a mission they believe in.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the holistic approach to autonomy support rather than just focusing on the language used in the emails. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration to gather sufficient data for meaningful analysis and to have a higher statistical level of confidence before drawing any conclusions.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of a donation with visual cues affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2023 - 03/18/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at or near the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a security “lock” icon to the button affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a security "lock" icon to the donation button resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the treatment showed a 122.8% increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.0%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid but can be considered directional with potential for future iterations.
However, the positive impact observed in this experiment suggests that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements may be effective in addressing donor hesitancy and increasing donation rates. Therefore, it would be advisable to continue testing and experimenting with different security enhancements in future campaigns to further validate their impact on donor conversion rates.
It is important to note that future experiments should consider a longer testing period to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. This will ensure that the results are more reliable and can be used for making informed decisions.
Overall, the key learning is that security-themed enhancements have the potential positively increase average gifts and overall revenue. Further experimentation is needed to verify these findings and determine the most effective security enhancements to implement in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of donation information with an impact statement beneath the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more prevalent security-themed statement below the donate button will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 04/01/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitant or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the security of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The inclusion of a security-themed statement below the donate button had a negative impact on donation rates, with a significant decrease in donations for the treatment.

2. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid due to a level of confidence below 85%, the consistently negative trends observed across multiple metrics and segments indicate that caution should be taken when considering the implementation of a security-themed statement in future experimentation.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to further investigate and address the underlying drivers of hesitancy or concerns among potential donors. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing the donation process, simplifying the user experience, and building trust through other means such as social proof, testimonials, or transparency in how donations are used.

Additionally, it may be valuable to conduct user research or surveys to better understand the reasons for hesitancy and gather feedback from potential donors. This qualitative data can provide insights into the specific concerns and help inform future experimentation and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How does using specificity and more active language that more closely relates to the mission in a donation page headline affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net’s main donation page will increase donation rates by better matching donor motivation and adding value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/07/2023
While Care Net’s homepage (which drives ~40% of traffic to the donation page over past 12 months), their mission, and even the gift array are focused largely on choice/options/alternatives for parents as a means to prevent abortion, this aspect is absent from the donation page headline, which is currently focused on saving lives, post Roe.
This could be causing confusion for potential donors as to “why to give” and a simple headline change may create a stronger “message match” and more incentive for potential donors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net's main donation page, did not have the intended effect of increasing donation rates. Instead, it resulted in a 28.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic was 88.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity. However, the results are approaching validity, meaning that this is a directional indicator that this result could happen again.

3. When looking at specific segments, the treatment had a significant negative impact on returning visitors, with a 583% decrease in donations and a 98% level of confidence. It also had a significant negative impact on visitors aged 45-54, with an 829% decrease in donations and a 99% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the treatment did not resonate with potential donors and had a negative impact on donation rates. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in the donation page headline to ensure it aligns with donor motivations and adds value. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the specific segments that were significantly affected, such as returning visitors and visitors aged 45-54, to better understand their preferences and tailor the messaging to their needs.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2023 - 04/28/2023
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high urgency campaigns. This year, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner led to a significant decrease in donor conversion for all traffic, with a 47.1% decrease.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 91.1%, which means that while it is not yet considered valid, it is approaching validity.

3. Returning visitors also saw a significant decrease in donations by 862% with a 98% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the addition of a gift array to the home page sticky banner did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion. It actually had a negative impact on donations, particularly for female visitors and returning visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be important to further investigate why the gift array had this negative impact and to explore alternative approaches that may be more effective in increasing donor conversion. This could involve conducting user research to better understand why certain segments of visitors were deterred from donating and using these insights to inform the design of future experiments.

It is also important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This indicates that the results may not be statistically significant and further testing is necessary to draw more conclusive findings. Therefore, we'd like to test this again during their fiscal year-end campaign in June.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a gift amount impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will communicating the impact each gift amount can have increase donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/27/2021
Care Net uses a traditional approach to their gift array buttons. This traditional approach simply has the gift amount on them. In an effort to increase donor conversion and, ultimately, overall revenue, we hypothesized that adding the impact of each specific gift amount might help motivate more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the way the gift array were presented and adding the impact of each specific amount on the button, we saw no difference in donor conversion. That said, we saw a directional lift in overall revenue by 22.8%! Additional here are some other observations from other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 186.0% increase in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve increase overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we nearly doubled the revenue from the campaign, we did not statistically validate the experiment due to an 88.6% level of confidence. With that said, this is certainly encouraging. When analyzing the individual gift, we did not see a decrease in overall donor average gift. Instead, we saw increases in the number of lower dollar donors and the higher dollar donors ($250+) maintained their previous level of giving.

It would be worth iterating on this concept but to validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve an increase in overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday, and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we came close to validating with a 91.2% level of confidence, the number of actual gifts was too small to determine whether this strategy was successful. To validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: Impact of reducing mental anxiety by adding, “cancel anytime” to a monthly donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that Adding “cancel anytime” language to our monthly donation page for visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2021 - 11/14/2021
We know that decreasing mental anxiety leads to an increase in donor conversion. One potential point of anxiety when asking for a monthly donation is the concern that the donor is then “locked into” a recurring transaction that they can’t get out of. We decided to test adding “cancel anytime” language to the donation page for our Fall monthly donor campaign to see which converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Due to the limited duration of the test (one two-week campaign) we couldn't validate the results on all audiences, however we had enough mobile traffic to validate based on that segment. Here, we found a 38% increase in donor conversion. Most other segments also reported an increase in donor conversion by adding the "cancel anytime language." ( the overall average was a 12% increase). This means that by reducing this mental anxiety we can convert more visitors into monthly donors. We will roll this out to other pages.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The impact of using a donor’s name on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seeing your name on a donation page after giving your name in a survey increase your liklihood to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2020 - 07/20/2020
As we continued to test the impact of using smart content on donation pages with DepositFix, we decided to take our high performing “Abortion in America Survey” Facebook campaign and test smart content on the donation page. Mainly, if someone was visiting the page already in our database, they would see their name on the page. Since they just gave their name on the survey, we thought that the continuity might increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one month, we achieved a 35% increase in donor conversion. However, our revenue decreased by 11%. This was due to a smaller average gift on the variation (smart content) page. The control page received two donations that were $250 and multiple that were $100.
With the lower revenue, we may not move forward with smart content on these campaigns. Revenue and donor conversion are both important, but ultimately revenue is the prevailing concern.

experiments.title: Does a personalized (smart content) donation page bring in more monthly donors from an email campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page containing personalized text perform better as part of an email donor campaign than a “static” donation page that was the same for each email recipient.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2020 - 04/04/2020
We have been looking at possibly switching to a donation platform that is built on Hubspot, allowing us to use smart content on the donation page (names, prior giving history, etc). Our goal being to reduce as much friction as possible.
The first test we launched of this new donor solution provided by DepositFix was to test our short monthly donor campaign. We were going to send 3 emails to our file seeking to activate 100 new monthly donors. For each of these emails, we split the list 50/50 and one set got a personalized email with a link to a static (non-smart) donation page in RaiseDonors, our current donor platform. The other half of the audience got a link to the smart donation page in DepositFix.
experiments.keyLearnings: In addition to a 50% higher donor conversion rate, the Deposit Fix page had an 8% higher average gift ($29.16 vs 26.97). While we didn’t quite hit 97% significance, the smart content page outperformed the static one in every email appeal of the campaign. We will continue testing this in future appeals.

experiments.title: The impact of using Facebook’s video creation tool on an ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a 15sec video created with the Facebook video creation tool convert more people into donors on the ad than a photo?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2019 - 08/11/2019
We’ve had a successful campaign advertising a pledge that led to a monthly donation page on Facebook. In a previous experiment, we found that using an image of a newborn baby on this offer significantly increased donor conversion (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2321)   We decided to test that image against a new video using the video creation tool in Facebook. They hypothesis was that a 15sec video would get more people to convert than the image.
experiments.keyLearnings: 218% more people who clicked the video ad became monthly donors than who clicked the photo ad. This was despite the fact that the photo ad had  14% more impressions. We will begin using and testing videos in more of our campaigns to see the impact they have on conversion.

experiments.title: The impact of making a monthly donor appeal in a homepage hero image (Hint: 1,392% increased revenue)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which would raise more money, a hero image making a one-time donation appeal, or one that would appeal for new monthly donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2019
Every two weeks, we would change out our homepage hero image featuring a donation CTA as well as our Top Ad donation CTA. We would swap these out to match the language of the most current bi-monthly digital appeal. These hero images were almost always for a one-time donation page.  We hypothesized that, instead of making a one-time donation appeal, we should try acquiring new monthly donors through the hero image. We did not set up a strict AB test, but rather replaced the hero and top ad images with a new monthly appeal and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Control had a higher donor conversion rate, but much fewer clicks. The variation attracted far more visitors to click and, since the appeal was for a monthly appeal, rather than a one-time appeal, resulted in significant more revenue. We ran the test for 72 days. Here are the results:
Control: 327 clicks, 13 donations, $981 raised.
Variation: 1057 clicks, 24 donations, $1,220 raised in monthly revenue, $14,640 in yearly revenue.
The variation had a 227% increase in clicks,  84% increase in donations, and a 1,392% increase in revenue! So, while fewer visitors to the donation page were prepared to become monthly donors, the significant increase in clicks and revenue more than made up for the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: The impact of adding a second CTA as a P.S. in email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. lead to a higher click-through rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2018
We decided to market our free online course to subscribers who had not yet enrolled. In crafting our emails, we wanted to know if adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. would lead to more people clicking through to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While our results were just below a 95% level of confidence, we saw a clear directional decrease in conversion with the addition of the P.S. Our hypothesis is that the P.S. did not feel natural to the flow and thus made the email seem less personal to the recipients.

experiments.title: Do images in an email next to blog links increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing images next to blog links in an email increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2018 - 05/01/2018
In the effort to continue to optimize the Weekend Update email, we wanted to see if further reducing the images in an email would impact the open and click rates of the send. Based on prior testing, we knew that reducing the image to text ratio of a message increased open rates significantly.
experiments.keyLearnings: Reducing the images in the weekend update to simply a small signature logo increased clicks by 10.4% and opens by 3.79%. Neither reached 95% significance, but both are close enough for a directional learning.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition on the acquisition and donation page impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a value proposition specific to the content offer help motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2018
Care Net developed a Lenten Devotional content offer and used Facebook to push people to their site to get the content offer. After people signed up for the content offer, they were then presented with an instant donation ask. This instant donation page used a value proposition that was Care Net focused.
