We hypothesized that having a donation page specific to the actual content offer might increase donor conversion. We created a treatment where the value proposition was specific to the Lenten Devotional and tested this against the control
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did see a significant directional lift with the treatment of 142%! This directional lift tells us that when we align our messaging with the value that people just experienced, we are able to motivate more people to take action and give.

experiments.title: What happens if we add a gift array to a sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array for the homepage sticky bar will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to start their process right there after saying yes instead of being directed to the donation page first.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Care Net recently ran a recurring campagin to raise money for their new merger ICU.
In the past, we have tested into adding a sticky bar to the homepage. For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions by adding the gift array to the sticky bar itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 5.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive result, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

 

It is interesting to note that while we saw no difference in the total number of transactions completed, we did find a 25% lift in total revenue at a 56% LoC and a 45% lift in recurring transactions completed at a 53% LoC. While the gift array could have made this impact. I think the CTA had stake at play too and could be giving us a hint at what the audience prefers. In the control we saw a CTA of "activate my monthly gift" where as the treatment had microgoal "be 1 of 50". I think it is important to look into this microgoal to see if we can test this hypothesis out.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar, but additional testing is needed to draw conclusive results. This information should be used to guide future experimentation and help refine strategies for increasing donations on the Care Net website.

experiments.title: Will a personalized slide out be more effective at conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask on the slidout for the content pages will achieve more thansactions because it feels more human.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Recently, Care net ran a recurring campaign with the goal to acquire 50 new monthly partners.
In an effort to support this campaign we added a slide out that had a direct ask on all content pages (read anything other than the homepage and donation page). In the past we have seen this slideout work really well.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we made the generalized ask a little more personal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized ask on the slideout for content pages resulted in a 35.5% decrease in donations compared to the generalized ask. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

 

When breaking down the results a little further we saw the control - without the personalized ask have an 80% increase in revenue and a 100% lift in completed recurring transactions. Which is what this campaign was trying to achieve.

Similar to the sticky bar, I think we're seeing these results due to the CTA that is on the buttons. It seems like the audiences responds better to a microgoal "become 1 of 50" vs. "activate your monthly gift".

It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with variations of personalized asks to see if there are specific types of personalization that resonate better with donors. Continuous experimentation and data analysis are essential in order to make informed decisions and optimize donation strategies for better results.

experiments.title: Are we presenting an acquisition offer in the best way on the landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the landing page for the Lent offer in MMS will achieve more form submissions because the page will be optimized for a mobile experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could change the landing page copy for the Lent offer in MMS and see more email sign ups.
We made several changes to the design of the page to make it more mobile friendly in terms of scrolling and skimming.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the adjusted landing page for the Lent offer in MMS did not result in more form submissions compared to the original page. Despite efforts to make the page more mobile-friendly for easier scrolling and skimming, the treatment actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 63.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test and iterate on the changes made to the landing page. It may be worth exploring different design elements, content, or CTAs to determine what truly resonates with the target audience and drives more form submissions. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision making.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience on the donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign cye donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the copy will be laid out in a way that makes it easier to digest and explains how the donor makes a difference.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For our CYE campaign, we wanted to see if having a clear guided reading experience on the main campaign donation page would influence people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign donation page had a positive impact on donor conversion rates, resulting in an 8.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% needed to consider the results as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to take into account the level of statistical confidence when interpreting results. While the increase in donations is promising, it is crucial to conduct further testing to validate the impact of the guided reading experience on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other aspects of the donation page and conduct A/B tests to optimize the layout, copy, and design for improved donor engagement and conversion. By continuing to experiment and gather data, Care Net can make more informed decisions to enhance its fundraising efforts and ultimately achieve greater success in driving donations for its campaigns.

experiments.title: Will having a specific donor goal increase the likelihood to give
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a specific goal that is counting down shows the social proof that other people are giving and provides inclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to see if having a specific micro goal would increase conversion on the homepage sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday did not increase donor conversion rates as hypothesized. The treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only at 31.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that there is a high level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the design of the sticky bar, the messaging used, or the timing of the campaign. It is also valuable to test different hypotheses and continue to learn from each experiment to optimize donation strategies in the future.

experiments.title: Will adding a humanized touch to an appeal increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making a humanized touch for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion because having the ask come from a specific person increases trust, and makes the ask a little more personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For the CYE homepage sticky bar, we wanted to see if adding an image of Roland on the ask and positioning it to be from Roland will convert donors better, rather than having just a regular ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Adding an image of Roland on the homepage sticky bar increased donations by 24.0%.

- The statistical level of confidence of 71.8% is below the recommended 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid.

- The hypothesis that a humanized touch would increase donor conversion was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Consider factors that may have influenced the lower statistical confidence, such as sample size or variability.

- Repeat the experiment with adjustments to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

- Continue exploring the idea of a humanized touch in donation asks, as it showed potential for increasing conversions.

experiments.title: What’s the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a campagin?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the homepage ask in a different way than a sticky bar for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the popup will draw a users eye directly to it, where the sticky bar could have banner blindness.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
In the past, Care Net was tested into using sticky bars on their homepage. Each campaign season we try to test adding a new elements to the sticky bar and messaging to see what is the best way to present it in order to get donors to convert. This year, we wondered if there was an opportunity to try something new to see if the sticky bar was still the best method. For this we created a slide up gift array that will show on the homepage that contains copy, and three specific ask amounts for people to start their donation on the homepage itself. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the popup gift array did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar for the CYE campaign. In fact, there was an 18.4% decrease in donations with a 39.8% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control (sticky bar) performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential for banner blindness with the sticky bar but also to acknowledge that new elements may not always outperform existing ones. It is crucial to thoroughly test any changes before fully implementing them, especially when the results do not reach a statistical level of confidence of at least 95%. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective method for increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding a humanized ask to a dear reader increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a picture of Roland for the CYE campaign dear readers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because their donor audience had previously responded well to personal asks from Roland.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
Earlier in our CYE campaign this year we found that adjusting the homepage sticky bar to be from a specific person increased conversions on the homepage. We wondered if the same would hold true for the dear readers.
For this test we created a dear reader that had the same copy and the same placement but added Roland’s image and signate to it. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment testing the addition of Roland's image to the CYE campaign dear reader showed a 32.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, compared to the control. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results are not considered valid for future decision making.

Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding Roland's image to the dear reader did not result in an increase in donor conversions, contrary to the hypothesis.

2. The previous success of personal asks from Roland on the homepage may not necessarily translate to other aspects of the website.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Conduct further tests with higher statistical confidence levels to draw more reliable conclusions.

2. Consider different approaches to personalization and messaging to determine what resonates best with the donor audience.

3. Continuously analyze and iterate on experimental results to enhance the effectiveness of donation strategies and optimize conversions.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial on the donation page reinforce the need to make a gift?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating. because  the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could get more people to become donors on the giving page by having a donor testimonial on the page itself.
Adding in extra value proposition to a donation page is not a new thing to test out, but with this test specifically we wanted to see if we could present it in a different way where the testimonial stands out and is able to convert more donors because it draws the eye to it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page could potentially lead to an increase in donor conversions. While the treatment did see a 3.0% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 13.2%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95% for validity.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials on donation pages, but with variations in how they are presented or combined with other elements to determine the most effective way to leverage social proof and value proposition. Additionally, it would be important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to generate statistically significant results, which can be used with confidence to inform decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to an acquisition donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donation page for the pledge will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because there will be clarity as to what the page is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2024 - 09/09/2024
Care net is a well-known organization that has multiple offers out in the market. One of these offerings is a pledge.
The offer is set up similar to any other, a user clicks on an ad, goes to the landing page, submits the form and then is redirected to a donation page. However, we were seeing a ton of people drop off once they hit the donation page. Could this be because they don’t want to give or is it because the page is not clear in what it is?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on the Pledge donation page are:

1. Changing the copy on the donation page can have a significant impact on donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group experienced a 22.2% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

3. The statistical confidence level of 60.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be reliable.

4. The hypothesis that clarity in the donation page copy will lead to higher conversion rates was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

1. Continue testing variations in copy and messaging on donation pages to optimize for clarity and donor conversion.

2. Ensure that experiments run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future hypotheses and testing strategies for donation pages across different campaigns and offerings.

4. Consider additional factors such as design, layout, and call-to-action language to further improve donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment are promising, it is important to conduct future experiments with a higher level of statistical confidence to make informed decisions for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding copy about giving monthly on a donation page impact recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy about why somebody should become a monthly donor for people who take the survey will achieve more recurring donations because it explains the value of becoming a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 05/08/2024
Care Net has a survey we were running in Facebook. The instant donation page for this offer didn’t have any language around why a donor should give monthly instead of once. We wanted to test how adding language about giving month would impact the recurring gifts made on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment version, which added copy about why someone should become a monthly donor, resulted in a 32.1% decrease in Recurring Donations for All Traffic with a 33.1% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the language used in donation appeals and how it may impact donor behavior. Further testing with different messaging strategies and potentially adjusting the copy to better communicate the value of becoming a monthly donor may help in identifying the most effective approach for increasing recurring donations.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated tabbed form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because there is additional clarity in the process by adding frequency choice before the gift array choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 03/18/2024
When sending pledge signers to an instant donation page, we wondered if emphasizing the monthly gift option by creating a tabbed form experience would increase recurring donor conversion. We split traffic between the two versions and ran for the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the animated tabbed form for the instant donation page resulted in a 50.3% increase in recurring donation for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As a result, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to determine the most effective ways to increase recurring donor conversion. It may be beneficial to explore other design and messaging variations in future experiments to see if they have a significant impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to navigation giving buttons impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons for the site navigation will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it increases the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 02/23/2024
Care Net tested into adding a “Give Monthly” button in their main navigation next to their main donate button. While this increased clarity, we wondered if we could increase the appeal of giving while adding even more clarity by adding additional copy below the main button text.
For the Donate button, we added the text, “Save a baby today.” For the Give Monthly button, we added the text, “Save babies 365 days a year.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons in the site navigation can potentially increase recurring donations by making the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis more prominent. The treatment, which added the text "Save a baby today" below the Donate button and "Save babies 365 days a year" below the Give Monthly button, resulted in a 67.0% increase in donations for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the treatment showed a promising outcome, it cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding additional copy below the main donation buttons to confirm if it consistently leads to increased donations. It is important to ensure that any findings have a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes based on the results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the copy or placement of the text could help optimize the impact on donation rates.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call-to-action impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the CTA up higher for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the CTA is more easily visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 12/31/2023
America Needs Fatima has previously tested a slide out vs. a dear reader to feature different content offers. For this test, we wanted to test the slide out method for a direct appeal ask vs. a traditional sticky bar to see if the appeal placement impacted overall donation conversions.
For this test, both web elements contained the same copy and button CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that placing the CTA higher on the website, using a slide out method, resulted in a 10.8% increase in one-time donations compared to a traditional sticky bar. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 64.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 85% to have valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other elements or factors that could impact donor conversions, such as the design or color scheme of the CTA button, the messaging used in the appeal, or the timing of the slide out or sticky bar. By testing these different elements and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, the organization can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/29/2023
This test is a follow-up test to the “How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion” test recorded earlier this year.
We wanted to retest this concept because this topic ended up being an integral part of their Fiscal Year End campaign.
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high-urgency campaigns. This year during their fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner resulted in a decrease in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid. But because this was at the end of Care Net's fiscal year, we ended up ending the experiment early and rolling out the control to ensure 100% of the traffic saw the better-performing variant.

Based on this, it is not recommended to use the gift array in the home page sticky banner for future campaigns. Instead, it would be worthwhile to explore other strategies or elements to improve donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on testing different approaches and measuring their impact on donor conversion to identify the most effective strategies for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the value proposition of a donation with an impact statement on the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving will increase donations by helping to overcome hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the impact of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving can potentially increase donations and help overcome hesitancy at the point of completing a transaction. Although the treatment showed a 19.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence of 72.3% is below the threshold of 85% needed for the results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving. The results of this experiment provide promising insights that can be further explored and tested with larger sample sizes to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, it is important to evaluate different variations of the statement to identify the most effective messaging to drive donations.

experiments.title: How updating value proposition on acquisition donation pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net’s mission will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
In the last few months, there have been some major social movements in the pro-life space, including the overturning of Roe v. Wade and the federal government announcing that they will be developing a Federal Reproductive Health Care Hotline. The hotline in particular is direct competition to one of Care Net’s ministries: the Pregnancy Decision Line. We wanted to see how updating the language to center around this particular issue would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net's mission resulted in a decrease in donor conversion and revenue. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on these learnings, it is important for Care Net to conduct further experimentation to validate the impact of updating the value proposition. The organization should explore other strategies and hypotheses to understand the factors contributing to the decrease in donor conversion. This can involve testing different messaging, design elements, or targeting strategies.

Future experimentation should be conducted for a longer duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical significance.

experiments.title: How does removing an inline image from an instant donation page affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an inline image on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by eliminating distractions, reducing friction, and focusing the reader’s attention on the content of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2023 - 03/09/2023
This organization has traditionally included inline images on its instant donation pages. We wanted to know if this might be creating friction by distracting the prospective donor from the appeal and interrupting their momentum to make a donation—or if the image was effectively working as a supporting element to add context and value to the appeal.
We wanted to test this hypothesis on a single offer to see if we could obtain a large enough sample size to attain statistically valid results before applying it to other offers. This experiment was set up as an A/B test with both the control (with image) and treatment (sans image) receiving roughly 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 24% lift in conversions by removing the inline image. However, these statistical results are deceiving because that 24% lift accounted for only one additional donation since the sample size was so low. The low sample size also prevented us from validating our hypothesis, producing only a 35% level of statistical confidence in these results.
Nevertheless, it is worth noting that desktop visitors had a 5.6% increase in donation rates, with a 96% level of confidence, which suggests that removing the inline image may be more effective for desktop users. Conversely, removing the image resulted in a 40% decrease in donations for mobile users, with a 61% level of confidence.
Due to the very small sample size achieved during this experiment, and because the inclusion of an inline image on instant donation pages is something this organization applies across the board, we are for now going to leave the image in place and test this hypothesis at a later date in the context of a revamped strategy to test our hypothesis across all instant donation pages in an effort to produce larger sample sizes and obtain more statistically reliable results.

experiments.title: How specificity in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You should read this with your family
You should read this to your kids

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specificity in the subject line increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 11/24/2020
Care Net was sending a cultivation email to their file and wanted to test the element of specificity in their subject line. The control was “You should read this with your family” and the treatment was, “You should read this with your kids”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift from the treatment. This tells us that the specificity from the treatment minimally helped increase engagement, but wasn't a significant enough difference to have a greater impact.

experiments.title: How communicating what the benefits are to the donor impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the donor benefits impact conversion on a monthly donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2020 - 12/07/2020
Care Net has a stand alone monthly gift acquisition donation page. In an effort to increase the number of recurring gifts and the overall revenue, they tested adding copy to the page that communicated tangible benefits for the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was only able to run for a short time before traffic to the page was no longer being pushed to it. But during the week the experiment ran, there was no negative impact on monthly donor conversion and we even saw promising data that there could be an increase in overall revenue. While we will want to test this again, these initial data points tells us that the donor benefit language could have a significant impact on overall revenue; people like and want to know what they will receive by becoming a monthly donor and increases motivation to give and give more generously.

experiments.title: How using communal language in an email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: How will communal language impact conversion in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/08/2021
Care Net found very positive results in a previous experiment with using communal language. This experiment was run on the homepage of their website. They wanted to test this similar approach in an email appeal to see if they would see a similar response through this donor theory. To test it, communal language was added to the call-to-action of the email appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language variation had no impact on click rate or donor conversion. This is due to the low engagement rate of the email. We will test this approach again in an email appeal and incorporate more communal language throughout the email to see if we can increase engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: Will smart content on a general donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will referring to visitors by their first name and featuring their past giving history on the donation page result in greater donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2020 - 04/26/2020
We have been exploring a new donation page solution provided by DepositFix. These pages are built within Hubspot, so we can use Hubspot’s smart content on the donation page to reference a visitors name, past giving, etc. We decided to test a radical redesign of our general donation page featuring the visitor’s name and the amount of their last donation vs our current “static” donation page provided by RaiseDonors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
According to Hubspot, no one converted on the smart variations of the Deposit Fix page. The donations we acquired were on the standard/non-smart version which was seen by visitors not in Hubspot. Our hypothesis is that people were turned off when they saw their prior gift/first name on the deposit fix page leading to few donations.
We think the reason that the smart content on the page worked when coming from an email campaign is because donors had just read a personalized email, so then seeing a personalized donation page “made sense” in a way seeing a personalized page while just browsing the public site didn’t. We will continue to test these two solutions to see what more we can learn.

experiments.title: The impact of using a campaign graphic in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “plain” email without the Giving Tuesday Now! graphic convert more donors than one with it?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2020 - 05/08/2020
For the international Giving Tuesday Now! campaign, we decided to test the impact of using the campaign’s graphic in our digital appeal for the event. We wanted to see if adding it would increase or decrease donor response
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the version without the graphic had a 23% higher CTR on the donation link than the one with the graphic. However, those who clicked on the link in the email with the graphic were 45% more likely to convert to donors, meaning that our overall donor and revenue conversion was 41% higher for the variation with the graphic than our normal “plain” template.
We want to do further tests as our sample size was too small for confidence, however it seems that there was the potential for a directional lift in these results.

experiments.title: How the addition of a dynamic sticky bar during a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation with the addition of a sticky bar on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2019 - 01/02/2020
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations from their primary donation page by including a sticky bar at the top of the page that reminded people how many donations they needed to reach their goal. The sticky bar was dynamic and would count down as donations were being made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sticky bar had no difference on donor conversion. These results were slightly surprising as we have seen this tactic help improve donor conversion for other organizations. That said, this experiment was performed during the highest giving season and donor motivation in a year. Our hypothesis is that we didn’t see a difference in conversion between the control and the treatment because donor motivation was already high. We’d like to test this same tactic when motivation is more neutral to see if we can create urgency during normal giving seasons.

experiments.title: Conversational “helpful” ad copy vs ad copy about a “war” on pro-life beliefs
experiments.researchQuestion: Which copy would convert more donors on our online course, copy that appealed to their feeling of being attacked by the media or copy that offered to help them overcome a longstanding fear?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2019 - 08/08/2019
We had a new online course, Defending Your Pro-Life Beliefs, that we wanted to advertise on Facebook. Our control ad focused on how pro-life beliefs are under attacks and how our course could help people defend their beliefs in the public square. We wanted to test this copy against copy that focused on how nervous people feel when they talk about being pro-life and how our course would help them be confident in these conversations. We wanted to see if appealing to the feeling among conservatives that their values were under attack would convert more donors than appealing to their desire to become confident.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more conversational copy won across the board. The variation had a click to course signup rate of 23% vs 17% for the control. It had a Click to donation rate of 1.34% vs 1.13%. During the two week test, the control brought in $462 while the variation brought in $700.
We found that people were more likely to take the course and donate when we explained in the ad copy how the course would help them feel confident, rather than when we appealed to their desire to protect themselves from the attacks of the pro-choice Left.

experiments.title: How indicating the time commitment of an email impacts open rate

rows.name: Why the "village" can't replace parents (4 min read)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the time commitment to the subject line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2018 - 11/10/2018
For the weekly cultivation email sent out by Care Net, we wanted to see if we could increase the email engagement by letting subscribers know the time commitment required to consume the topic. This is something often seen on blog posts but we wanted to see if it would have a positive impact in email. To do this, we added ” (4 min read)” to the end of the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a nearly identical open rate between the two versions. After further reflection, it is our hypothesis that the longer subject line may have resulted in the “(4 min read)” not being shown in the inbox. Having this aspect cut off would have resulted in the two variants being identical.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: Dear Reader footer ad vs a mid-page pledge ad (500% increase)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will bring in more monthly donors, the dear reader footer ad or the pledge ad?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2019
In a prior test, we found that we could significantly increase the revenue from our Life News ads by creating a footer ad that looked like part of the site, using “dear reader” language on the ad, and linking it to a pledge and then to a monthly donation page. (the results of that test are here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2423) The “dear reader” ad had higher conversion and higher revenue than the pledge ad, but the pledge got more clicks. We wondered if we would bring in more monthly donors on the pledge due to its higher volume of clicks if we changed it so the pledge ad linked to the same monthly donor campaign as the dear reader one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the dear reader maintained a higher click to donation rate (16.67%), the pledge ad had 627% more traffic. This caused us to bring in 500% more monthly donors on the pledge ad than the “dear reader” footer ad. We also had a 76% higher email acquisition rate on traffic to the pledge ad vs the dear reader.
We aren’t sure how much of these results is due to the higher placement on the page of the pledge ad. We will likely test a “dear reader” version of this mid-page placement in the future.

experiments.title: How indicating the number of steps for a single-step funnel impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding clarity by adding “step 1 of 2” above the form increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2018 - 11/15/2018
We tested adding “step 1 of 2” on our pro-life 101 free online course landing page and increased conversion by 22%. We wanted to test this on another offer and see if it had similar impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we were unable to validate. There was a slight increase, however it was not significant. We will continue adding the text to our landing pages, but any impact will probably be contingent on the specific offer and how traffic was driven there.

experiments.title: How using a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the Dear Reader feature at the end of articles on the Care Net blog. Our effort to do this included making it more personal by adding a signer to the appeal and making it more urgent and relevant by including year-end focused language and a countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the changes we made had little to no impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this might be due to the motivation of the user. We launched this campaign towards the end of the month when donor motivation is much higher. When donor motivation is higher, we normally see that our effort to increase donor conversion doesn’t work or has no impact. We propose testing a similar approach when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: Impact of using a baby image in a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Do images of babies perform better than images of pregnant women.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
We have an MLD pledge we use to introduce people to MLD and ask them to become volunteers. We wanted to increase the number of volunteers that sign-up. We knew that baby images were performing well in other tests, so we decided to try it in this Facebook ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: The image of the baby performed significantly better than the image of the pregnant woman. Next, we will test an image of a fetus against this baby image to see if it performs better.

experiments.title: The Impact of Making Two CTAs in one Appeal Pt. 1
experiments.researchQuestion: Would appealing to donors to “cut the cord” by cutting their entertainment bill (and its support of pro-choice programming) in favor of becoming a monthly donor increase the number of donations we received? What would the impact of making two CTAs in the same appeal be on donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2018
For months, Care Net has been brainstorming ways to increase the number of monthly donors, Caring Partners, that we have on file. We proposed testing our traditional appeal language against a new “Cut the Cord” campaign. This appeal would argue that money spent on entertainment providers like Netflix, Amazon Prime, Cable often supports pro-choice initiatives. By “cutting” some of our expenses on such entertainment and redirecting the money to Care Net’s work, lives would be saved and pro-choice orgs would lose funding. A win-win. We hoped that a pro-life person’s ire about pro-choice funding and programming would help motivate them to “cutting the cord” and saving lives.
The drawback to this approach would be that it would be essentially making two calls to actions: Cut expenses on entertainment and become a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the Cut the Cord email had the benefit of a new sender and a higher CTR, the donation rate was significantly lower. The results seemed to confirm that two CTAS in the appeal suppressed donor response as they had to agree to cutting back on something they were already doing and beginning something they weren’t yet doing. This was too much friction. However, we wanted to be sure. The variation had a 33% higher CTR. We wondered if we had too much copy on the donation page for the variation and that people dropped off who were prepared to make a gift. We decided to run an additional test to see if shorter copy on the donation page would cause Cut the Cord to perform better.

experiments.title: How changing the layout of an acquisition page impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would laying the page out differently help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
After a person signs a pro-life pledge, they are taken to a confirmation page for the pledge they just signed that is also an acquisition page for the Pro-life 101 online course acquisition page. Care Net hypothesized that simplifying the copy on that page into a stepped approach might help increase conversion of the online course. They developed a treatment that laid out the same copy from the control differently in an effort to make the page easier to read.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for over two weeks, there was no significant difference in conversion and had a low level of confidence. What this tells us is that a simplified, stepped approach has no real impact on this page and that a more radical redesign is needed to effect conversion.

experiments.title: How asking for a recurring gift vs. a single gift affects conversion in a “Dear Reader” experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for a recurring donation affect conversion in a Dear Reader experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/25/2018
Through previous testing, Care Net found that by inserting a “Dear Reader” feature at the end of their blog posts, they were able to increase the number of people to their donation page. During the month of January, Care Net made an asserted effort to acquire more recurring donors. To support this effort, they tested adding a recurring focused ask in the “Dear Reader” feature instead of a one-time donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: You can see in the data that we weren’t able to validate the experiment in the short time that we ran it. We needed to turn off this experiment earlier than planned resulting in a small sample size. Because the Dear Reader feature gets more people to the donation page, we recommend testing this recurring gift focus again because of the potential revenue impact it could have.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts name conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2018
When evaluating the performance of the Pro-Life 101 online course, Care Net flagged the acquisition page as the biggest opportunity for optimization in the funnel based off of it’s conversion rate. They had been running ads that featured an image of a family. This same image was also featured on their acquisition page. After testing different images with their Facebook ads, we found that an image of a fetus increased click-through on the ads and ultimately people signing up for the course. They hypothesized that creating a radical redesign of the acquisition page featuring the same image of the fetus from the Facebook ad, changing and strengthening the value proposition and using testimonials of people who had already taken the course might help increase conversion. They A/B split the traffic coming to the page and tested this radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After the running the experiment for over a month and only reaching a 20%, there wasn’t a significant difference between the control and treatment. This suggests that a radical redesign of the page doesn’t impact conversion. That said, because the test had low power as well, we would need a much larger sample size the validate that claim. We propose testing more specific elements on the page to both increase conversion and understand the audience better.

experiments.title: How putting a countdown clock in the body copy of an email appeal impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a countdown clock increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2017 - 01/31/2018
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase motivation of their email file to click through to their donation page towards the end of the campaign by increasing urgency in the email appeal. To do this, they inserted an animated countdown clock in the body of their email copy and tested this against the control version that didn’t have the clock. The clock counted down to the end of the year and informed the end reader that their time was running out to make their year-end donation. All other copy remained the same. They split their file in half and tested this approach
experiments.keyLearnings: White we weren’t able to validate the experiment, there was a definite directional lift with the treatment of 54%. By adding a countdown clock to the copy, we were able to increase urgency to the message and motivate more people to click-through to the donation page. This tactic will be implemented moving forward for high-urgency campaigns in an effort to not only get significantly more people to click through to the donation page, but potentially give to their organization.

experiments.title: How using clarity in the call-to-action can impact traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving button copy would increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2017 - 10/11/2017
Care Net wanted to increase the volume of traffic going to their primary donation page. One of the ways to try and do this is to test the kind of copy that was being used on the “donate” button of their homepage. Their current donate button currently used value proposition focused language, “Save Babies Now”. They hypothesized that a treatment version with language that more directly reflected where the button would take them once they clicked on it might increase traffic to the donation page and ultimately have a positive impact on donations and revenue. They treatment button copy said “Support Care Net”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy had a slight increase in conversion, but the results weren’t statistically valid. The experiment ran for over a month and would need a more radical treatment with a larger sample size to validate.
Even though the test didn’t validate, we can hypothesize that with the slight increase in conversion from the button that said “Support Care Net”, the copy presented more clarity with the end-users action. They knew exactly where they would be taken if they clicked on the button. By saying “Save Babies Now” in the control, the copy may have been too vague and created a higher level of friction in the mind of the end-user – where would they be taken if they clicked on that button? What if they weren’t ready to “save a baby now”?
When we test copy in a call-to-action, it is good to keep in mind that clarity trumps persuasion.
